title: Goat hoof trimming
subtitle: How to trim overgrown hooves and identify early foot problems.
excerpt: A goat's hooves grow continuously. On soft or wet ground they overgrow quickly, folding under and trapping mud and bacteria. Regular trimming every 6 to 8 weeks is quicker and easier than recovering a seriously overgrown foot.
sourceNotes: NADIS (National Animal Disease Information Service), goat foot care factsheet. British Goat Society, hoof care guidance. SAC Consulting, goat health management notes.

content.text: Goats kept on soft pasture or housed on bedding need trimming more often than those on rough, stony ground, which wears the hoof naturally. A goat with properly maintained hooves stands with its weight evenly distributed across all four feet and moves without hesitation.

A milking stand with a headstock makes trimming easy and safe for both the goat and the handler. Without a stand, secure the goat to a post at head height with a short rope and work from the side. Never trim a goat that is struggling violently; wait until it settles, or pen it briefly with hay to distract it before starting.
What a correctly trimmed hoof looks like
The sole should be flat and the

should be parallel to the coronary band (the skin-hoof junction at the top). The toe should not protrude beyond the heel when viewed from the side. The two claws should be roughly even in size. The central cleft between the claws should be clean and open.

Remove loose or flapping pieces of wall first. Then trim the overgrown toe, working from the tip back in thin slices parallel to the coronary band. Stop when the sole starts to show a pale, waxy appearance; this is close to the live tissue. Trim the inner and outer walls level with the sole. Clean out the cleft between the claws with the tip of the hoof trimmer. The finished hoof should sit flat on the ground with no rocking.

If you cut into the live tissue and the hoof bleeds, stop trimming that foot. Apply firm pressure with a clean cloth for a few minutes. The goat will be uncomfortable but a single nick is not a serious injury. If the bleeding does not stop within 10 minutes, apply a blood-stop powder or clean bandage and check the foot for heat or swelling over the following days.
If when trimming you find soft, grey, foul-smelling tissue beneath the wall, or the wall separates from the sole with minimal pressure, this is

. Isolate the animal, clean the hoof, and contact a vet; foot rot requires antibiotic treatment prescribed by a vet and is notifiable to the flock if multiple animals are affected. Do not walk infected animals through shared footbathing if you have unaffected animals.
